content: Identify the wrong relation for real gases :
explanation:
Wrong relation about real gases is given in option a. Its correct form is

$$Z=\frac{V_{\text {real }}}{V_{\text {idea }}}$$

( $Z=$ Compressibility factor)

content: Which property of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ makes it biologically and geo-chemically important?
explanation: The property of low solubility of carbon dioxide in water makes it biologically and geo-chemically important. Carbon dioxide on reaction with water form carbonic acid, which dissociates to give $$\mathrm{HCO}_3^{-}$$ ions. $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 / \mathrm{HCO}_3^{-}$$ buffer system helps to maintain $$\mathrm{pH}$$ of blood between $$7.26-7.42$$.

content: The volume of $$2.8 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{CO}$$ at $$27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ and 0.821 atm pressure is ($$R=0.08210 \text { L. atm K} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$)
explanation:
Given, amount of $$\mathrm{CO}=2.8 \mathrm{~g}$$

Temperature $$=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$

Pressure $$=0.821 \mathrm{~atm}$$

$$R=0.08210 \mathrm{~L~atm} \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$

We know that, $$p V=R T$$

$$\therefore \quad V=\frac{R T}{p}=\frac{0.0821 \times 300}{0.821}=30 \mathrm{~L}$$

$$\therefore 28 \mathrm{~g}$$ of CO occupy volume $$30 \mathrm{~L}$$

$$\therefore 2.8 \mathrm{~g}$$ of CO occupy volume $$=\frac{30 \times 2.8}{28}=3 \mathrm{~L}$$

content: When the absolute temperature of ideal gas is doubled and pressure is halved, the volume of gas

options.content: will be half of original volume
will be 4 times the original volume
will be 2 times the original volume
will be 1/4th times the original volume

explanation:
Given, final temperature $$\left(T_2\right)$$

$$=2 \times\left(T_1\right) \text { initial temperature}$$

Final pressure $$\left(p_2\right)=2 \times \frac{\left(p_1\right)}{2}$$ initial pressure

According to gas law,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1} & =\frac{p_2 V_2}{T_2} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1} & =\frac{p_1 \times V_2}{2 \times 2 T_1} \\ V_2 & =4 V_1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Volume increase by 4 times.

content: Which of the following equations does not represent Charles's law for a given mass of gas at constant pressure?
explanation:
Charle's law states that "pressure remaining constant, the volume of a fixed mass of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature. The general equation is as follows :

$$\frac{V}{T}=\text { Constant }(K) ; \quad V=K T\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Taking the logarithm of equation (i)

$$\begin{aligned} & \log V=\log K+\log T \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & \log K=\log V-\log T \quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Equation (ii) can also be represented as

$$\ln V=\ln K+\ln T \quad \text{.... (iv)}$$

Taking differentiation of equation (iv) w.r.t $$T$$.

$$\frac{d(\ln V)}{d T}=0+\frac{1}{T} ; \frac{d(\ln V)}{d T}=\frac{1}{T}$$

content: For an ideal gas, compressibility factor is
explanation:
The compressibility factor, denoted by $$Z$$, is defined as:

$$Z = \frac{PV}{nRT}$$

For an ideal gas, the ideal gas law states that:

$$PV = nRT$$

Substituting this into the expression for $$Z$$ gives:

$$Z = \frac{nRT}{nRT} = 1$$

So, the compressibility factor for an ideal gas is 1.

Answer: Option B.

Dry ice is the solid form of carbon dioxide. Here's a quick explanation:

Dry ice is made of carbon dioxide ($$CO_2$$).

At atmospheric pressure, carbon dioxide does not melt into a liquid but instead transitions directly from a solid to a gas; this process is called sublimation.

Among the options provided, the correct answer is the one that indicates dry ice is a solid form of carbon dioxide.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option C: solid $$CO_2$$

content: The pressure of real gases is less than that of ideal gas because of

options.content: increases in the kinetic energy of the molecules
increases in the number of collisions

explanation: The presence is much greater in ideal gas compared to the pressure of a real gas since the particles do not have the attractive forces that enable the molecules to hold back when they will collide at an impact. Hence, particles collide with less energy.
